content.text: An OKR (Objectives and Key Results) cascade connects the GenAI programme's work to the organisation's strategic direction. Without a cascade, teams can be busy — even productive — on work that does not move the metrics the business actually cares about. The cascade makes the line of sight explicit: every team-level key result traces upward to a programme objective, which traces upward to a company objective.
The cascade test: if a team cannot draw a two-hop line from its key result to a company objective, the work is either misaligned or the cascade is broken. Both must be fixed.

The three-level cascade shown is typical for a GenAI programme embedded in a larger organisation. The company sets 3–5 annual objectives; the GenAI programme contributes 2–4 of its own objectives that support those; teams contribute quarterly objectives that deliver against the programme objectives. Every key result at the team level must trace upward within two hops.
Writing Good OKRs for GenAI Programmes
GenAI OKRs fail in two common ways:
('ship an AI assistant') that measure output rather than outcome, and
('improve developer experience') that cannot be objectively scored at quarter end. The discipline is:
: a qualitative, inspirational direction statement. It answers 'where are we going this quarter?' — not 'what will we build?'
: a quantitative, time-bounded measure. It answers 'how will we know we arrived?' Must have a specific number and a data source.
Reduce document review time for the legal team
Model precision ≥ 92 % on the internal eval set by Oct 1
Time-to-first-summary < 4 s at p95 for 1,000 concurrent users
40 % of eligible tasks completed via AI (vs manual), week-over-week stable
Data Model for a GenAI OKR Cascade
Building the Cascade — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up
There are two approaches to building the cascade, and the right answer depends on where your organisation starts:

The company or division sets annual objectives first. The GenAI programme team then writes programme objectives that are explicitly justified by their contribution to ≥ 1 company key result. Teams then write their objectives justified by programme objectives. This is the ideal flow — every team OKR has a clear upstream mandate.

If the programme already has ongoing work, use bottom-up: inventory all current team objectives, then trace them upward. Any team objective that cannot be mapped to a programme objective within two rounds of 'why does this matter?' is a candidate for deprioritisation or re-framing. This is a useful audit exercise even in mature organisations.
OKRs are set at the start of a quarter and reviewed at four check-in points. Each check-in produces a status update and — if any KR is off-track — a corrective action item.
Confirm parent-child links; baseline all KR current values
RAG status per KR; flag at-risk items
For off-track KRs: root cause + corrective action plan
Final scores; retrospective; feed into next-quarter cascade

At quarter close, each key result is scored on a 0.0–1.0 scale. OKR literature (Google's approach) uses 0.7 as the 'green' threshold for stretch KRs — consistently hitting 1.0 means targets were not ambitious enough. For GenAI programmes, calibrate based on team maturity: early-stage teams should target 0.6–0.8; mature delivery teams should target 0.7–0.9.
: more than 3 objectives per level dilutes focus. If everything is a priority, nothing is.
KRs that are tasks, not outcomes
: 'Complete model evaluation' is a task. 'Model F1 score ≥ 0.88 on held-out test set' is an outcome.
: if the current value of a KR cannot be read from a system automatically, it will not be tracked honestly. Designate the source at OKR creation.
: OKRs set and forgotten. Off-track KRs discovered at quarter-end have no time for correction.
: targets are too easy. A well-designed GenAI OKR should have meaningful stretch — teams hitting 0.7–0.85 consistently are calibrated correctly.
: teams write OKRs that reflect their preferred work rather than programme priorities. The cascade alignment check (

GenAI programmes frequently involve shared infrastructure and data pipelines — which means one team's key result depends on another team delivering on time. These cross-team dependencies must be made explicit in the cascade, not just assumed.

Map cross-team dependencies at the start of each quarter alongside the OKRs. Any blocking dependency on an at-risk upstream KR should trigger an immediate conversation between team leads — not wait for the week-9 check-in.
The intake and scoring system assigns approved projects to the portfolio; the OKR cascade defines what the portfolio must achieve. The connection point is the

: each committed project should contribute to at least one key result. This mapping serves two purposes: it makes the case for why a project is funded, and it provides the leading indicator data that populates OKR progress during the quarter.

If a key result has no project contributing to it, either a project was missed during portfolio planning or the KR is aspirational without an execution path. Either way it needs resolution before the quarter starts.

A GenAI programme OKR cascade connects team-level delivery to company strategy through a two-hop chain: team objectives support programme objectives, which support company objectives. Every key result must be quantitative, time-bounded, and sourced from a real data system. The cascade is validated at quarter start (alignment check for orphan objectives), checked at weeks 2, 5, and 9, and scored at week 13 on a 0.0–1.0 scale with 0.7 as the 'green' threshold. Common failures — too many objectives, task-based KRs, absent mid-quarter reviews, and sandbagged targets — all undermine the cascade's ability to focus and evaluate the programme.
